one_line_takeaway: In a controlled OPT-1.3B/C4 benchmark, back-translation degrades all four evaluated LLM watermarks (KGW, SIR, Unbiased, EWD) far more than paraphrasing does, with Unbiased the most paraphrasing-robust (AUC 0.87) and SIR the most back-translation-robust (AUC 0.67) but weakest under paraphrasing (AUC 0.64), while KGW collapses to near-chance (AUC 0.52) under back-translation despite also causing the largest stylistic distortion (Levenshtein 823, shortest sentences).
key_finding: No-perturbation AUC (near-ceiling for all): SIR 0.99, EWD 0.98, Unbiased 0.97, KGW 0.95. Under paraphrasing, AUC dropped for all methods: Unbiased 0.87 (best), KGW 0.80, EWD 0.71, SIR 0.64 (worst). Under back-translation, degradation was more severe for all: SIR 0.67 (best under this attack, a reversal from its paraphrasing rank), EWD 0.61, Unbiased 0.59, KGW 0.52 (near chance). Levenshtein distance from original: KGW 823 (largest edit distance/most aggressive rewriting), EWD 600, Unbiased 375, SIR 127 (smallest, most faithful to surface form). Mean sentence length (words): Original 24.50, SIR 24.38, EWD 22.20, Unbiased 21.80, KGW 19.57 (shortest). Sentiment skew: Original 47% negative/35% neutral/18% positive; SIR skewed most sharply to 68% negative; KGW showed highest positive sentiment at 27%; Unbiased was closest to Original's neutral balance (42% neutral). Pearson correlations with prediction correctness: average sentence length r=0.91, average word length r=0.83, total text length r=0.73, noun frequency (NN) r=0.71, determiners (DT) r=0.69, past-tense verbs (VBD) r=0.63; positive sentiment r=-0.75 (strong negative correlation with correct/robust detection), neutral sentiment r=-0.08, negative sentiment r=0.56. The paper's overall claims: back-translation strips watermark signals more effectively than paraphrasing across all four techniques; Unbiased and SIR best preserve linguistic style/fluency (smallest Levenshtein distance and/or closest sentiment/POS match to original) while KGW and EWD introduce more pronounced stylistic distortion; longer, syntactically denser, less positively-toned text is associated with more robust (harder to evade) watermarking.
core_contribution: An empirical benchmark study (not a new method) that evaluates four existing LLM text-watermarking algorithms -- KGW, SIR, Unbiased, and EWD -- side by side under the same pipeline (MarkLLM, generation model Facebook OPT-1.3B) along two axes simultaneously: (1) robustness of watermark detection to paraphrasing and back-translation adversarial attacks, measured via ROC-AUC, and (2) preservation of linguistic quality/style (POS-tag distributions, sentiment, Levenshtein distance from the original, sentence/word/text-length statistics). It then runs a Pearson correlation analysis between the linguistic features and detector prediction correctness (correct/incorrect) to identify which stylistic properties are associated with watermark survivability, arguing this explains why some algorithms are more attack-resistant than others.
threat_model: Attacker goal: evade LLM-generated-text watermark detection while preserving the semantic content of the text (i.e., strip the statistical watermark signal without destroying meaning). Attacker capability: black-box access to the watermarked text only (no access to the watermarking key, green/red list, or detection model); attacks are applied post-hoc to already-generated watermarked text using two off-the-shelf LLM-based transformations -- (a) paraphrasing via LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct, and (b) back-translation (English->French->English) via Multilingual LLaMA-3-8B. Defender: the watermark detector (WatermarkedTextDetectionPipeline / UnWatermarkedTextDetectionPipeline in MarkLLM) trying to classify text as watermarked vs. not, scored by ROC-AUC. No adaptive/white-box attacker, no decoding-time attacks (temperature/nucleus tuning), no prompt injection, and no adversarial fine-tuning were evaluated (explicitly listed as limitations by the authors).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) all linguistic evaluation restricted to a cleaned English subset of C4 -- no code, dialogue, multilingual, or noisy-text domains tested; (2) single generation model (OPT-1.3B in the methodology text, though the limitations section says 'OPT-13B' -- an inconsistency within the paper itself) -- no cross-architecture check on LLaMA-3, Mistral, DeepSeek, or Phi; (3) watermark coverage limited to what MarkLLM implements (4 algorithms), not exhaustive of the watermarking literature; (4) adversarial scope restricted to paraphrasing and back-translation only -- no decoding-manipulation attacks (temperature/nucleus sampling), prompt injection, or adversarial fine-tuning attacks were tested; (5) linguistic analysis limited to surface-level token/frequency features (POS, sentiment, Levenshtein, length stats) -- syntactic depth, discourse structure, semantic coherence, formality, hedging, and lexical diversity were not analyzed. Additionally (reviewer-noted, not paper-stated): the study is a benchmarking/evaluation exercise, not a new watermarking or attack method, so it makes no methodological contribution beyond the comparative measurement; the Pearson correlation analysis (RQ3) pools results across all four algorithms together, which may conflate per-algorithm effects since attack robustness and text-generation behavior differ substantially by algorithm; correlation is reported without significance testing or confidence intervals.

datasets_used: C4 (Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus), English subset, ~10,223 prompts, 50-500 words per passage
quantitative_results: No-perturbation AUC: SIR 0.99, EWD 0.98, Unbiased 0.97, KGW 0.95
Paraphrasing-attack AUC: Unbiased 0.87, KGW 0.80, EWD 0.71, SIR 0.64
Back-translation-attack AUC: SIR 0.67, EWD 0.61, Unbiased 0.59, KGW 0.52
Levenshtein distance from original text: KGW 823, EWD 600, Unbiased 375, SIR 127
Mean sentence length (words): Original 24.50, SIR 24.38, EWD 22.20, Unbiased 21.80, KGW 19.57
Mean word length (chars): Original 4.44, SIR 4.42, EWD 4.28, Unbiased 4.31, KGW 4.17
Mean text length (chars): Original 1403, SIR 1370, Unbiased 1345, EWD 1328, KGW 1281
Sentiment (Negative/Neutral/Positive %): Original 47/35/18, SIR 68/20/12, KGW 44/29/27, Unbiased 36/42/22, EWD 43/39/18

Pearson r with detector prediction correctness: avg sentence length 0.91, avg word length 0.83, total text length 0.73, NN 0.71, DT 0.69, VBD 0.63, NNS 0.62, positive sentiment -0.75, negative sentiment 0.56, neutral sentiment -0.08
baselines_compared: SIR - Semantic Invariant Robust watermark (Liu et al. 2024a)
EWD - Entropy-based Watermark Detection (Lu et al. 2024)
